keyIdeas: Atoms consist of a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons in shells.
Relative charge: proton = +1, neutron = 0, electron = −1. Relative mass: proton ≈ 1, neutron ≈ 1, electron ≈ 1/1840 (negligible).
Atomic (proton) number Z: the number of protons in the nucleus; defines the element.
Mass (nucleon) number A: the total number of protons and neutrons (nucleons) in the nucleus.
In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons (Z).

mustKnow.definition: The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom; determines the element.
The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.
Proton: +1; neutron: 0; electron: −1 (measured relative to the proton charge).
Proton: 1; neutron: 1; electron: 1/1840 ≈ 0 (all relative to the mass of a proton).

markSchemePhrases: proton number = atomic number = number of protons
relative mass: proton 1, neutron 1, electron negligible
commandWords: 'State' the relative charge or mass of a subatomic particle — give the numerical value. 'Calculate' the number of neutrons from Z and A. 'Identify' the element from a given Z value.
examinerTips: Know the table of relative charges and masses — it is commonly tested as a 1-mark recall.
Nucleon number (A) is always the larger number in nuclide notation ²³⁵₉₂U; atomic number (Z) is the smaller.
Neutrons = A − Z — a trivial calculation but frequently tested in 1-mark questions.
A neutral atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons; ions have gained or lost electrons but the number of protons is unchanged.
At A-level, avoid GCSE phrasing: say 'proton number' not 'atomic number' (both are acceptable but proton number is more precise).
workedExample:
An atom of an element has mass number 56 and proton number 26. State (i) the element, (ii) the number of neutrons, (iii) the number of electrons in a neutral atom.

(i) Proton number 26 = Iron (Fe)
(ii) Neutrons = A − Z = 56 − 26 = 30
(iii) Neutral atom: electrons = protons = 26
commonTraps: Confusing mass number and atomic number — remember Z (atomic/proton number) is always the subscript in nuclide notation.
Saying the neutron has a slight positive charge — it is neutral (charge = 0).
Forgetting that the electron mass is negligible, not zero — write 'approximately 1/1840' or 'negligible' rather than '0'.
Stating the atomic number changes in an ion — the number of protons is fixed; only electrons change when ions form.
